original_abstract: espanolEste articulo examina de una manera critica la politica europea de incluir clausulas de elementos esenciales en acuerdos comerciales, como principal medio para la realizacion del nexo comercio-derechos humano exigido por la obligacion de la UE de articular sus relaciones externas para la promocion de sus valores fundamentales. Despues de estudiar la anatomia de dichas clausulas, el articulo se propone analizar las criticas que estas clausulas han generado en relacion con (i) el hecho de que no todos los acuerdos comerciales de la UE contienen estas clausulas, (ii) su redaccion y alcance, y (iii) su evaluacion e implementacion. El articulo concluira con una valoracion de la efectividad y legitimidad de esta politica y de la manera en la que influye sobre la credibilidad del programa normativo de las relaciones exteriores de UE. EnglishThis article critically examines the EU policy of including essential elements clauses in trade agreements as the principal avenue for the realization of the trade-human rights nexus required by the EU�s treaty obligation to use its external relations for the promotion of its core values. After evaluating the anatomy of such clauses, the article sets out to analyze the criticism which they have generated regarding (i) the fact that such clauses are not contained in all EU trade agreements, (ii) their drafting and scope, and (iii) their monitoring and enforcement. The article then concludes with an assessment of the effectiveness and legitimacy of this policy, and of the way it impacts the credibility of the EU�s normative external relations agenda.
humanized_abstract: Spanish The aim of this article is to assess EU policy with regard to the inclusion of essential elements clauses in EU trade agreements, which is the main way in which the trade-human rights nexus mandated by Article 205(2) TFEU is fulfilled. After outlining the structure of the clauses at issue, the article will examine the criticisms raised with respect to a series of issues: firstly, the fact that not all EU trade agreements contain essential elements clauses; secondly, the wording and scope of these clauses; and thirdly, the assessment and implementation of their monitoring and enforcement. Finally, the article will evaluate the effectiveness and legitimacy of the EU's policy regarding essential elements clauses, while analysing their impact on the credibility of the EU's normative external relations programme. English The EU policy of including essential elements clauses in its trade agreements is the main way of realizing the trade-human rights nexus mandated by Article 205(2) TFEU which requires the Union to use its external relations activity to promote its founding values. After analyzing the structure of the essential elements clauses, this paper examines the criticisms leveled in respect of the following issues: - (i) that not all of the EU's trade agreements include an essential elements clause; - (ii) that these clauses are not formulated in the same way, which raises questions as to their scope and effectiveness; - (iii) their implementation, that is the modalities of their monitoring and enforcement. The article ends with an assessment of the effectiveness and legitimacy of EU policy on essential elements clauses, with a particular focus on their impact on the credibility of the Union's normative external relations agenda.

Finally, the article will evaluate the effectiveness and legitimacy of the EU's policy regarding essential elements clauses, while analysing their impact on the credibility of the EU's normative external relations programme. The article ends with an assessment of the effectiveness and legitimacy of EU policy on essential elements clauses, with a particular focus on their impact on the credibility of the Union's normative external relations agenda.
